title: MapReduce: Simplified Data Processing on Large Clusters
author: Jeffrey Dean and Sanjay Ghemawat (Google)
note: The original OSDI 2004 paper: map and reduce functions over key/value pairs, with the runtime handling partitioning, scheduling, machine failures, and inter-machine communication.

title: Command-line Tools can be 235x Faster than your Hadoop Cluster
author: Adam Drake
note: A find | xargs mawk | mawk pipeline crunches 1.75 GB of chess data in ~12 seconds (~270 MB/sec) versus 26 minutes on Hadoop — a case study in Unix-pipeline batch processing.

title: Resilient Distributed Datasets: A Fault-Tolerant Abstraction for In-Memory Cluster Computing
author: Matei Zaharia et al. (UC Berkeley)
note: The NSDI 2012 Spark paper: RDDs achieve fault tolerance via lineage of coarse-grained transformations instead of replicating intermediate state to disk like MapReduce.

title: RDD Programming Guide
author: Apache Spark project
note: Official Spark guide to transformations vs. actions, lazy evaluation, persistence, and why ByKey and join operations trigger shuffles across partitions.

title: Performance Tuning (Spark SQL)
author: Apache Spark project
note: Documents broadcast vs. sort-merge vs. shuffle-hash join strategies, join hints, and Adaptive Query Execution's automatic skew-join splitting — the joins taxonomy from this chapter in practice.

title: Building and operating a pretty big storage system called S3
author: Andy Warfield (AWS, on Werner Vogels' blog)
note: An S3 VP explains object-store internals: spreading objects across millions of disks for heat management and Reed-Solomon erasure coding into identity and parity shards.

title: Dags — Apache Airflow Documentation
author: Apache Airflow project
note: Official reference for orchestrating batch workflows as directed acyclic graphs of tasks with dependencies, schedules, and trigger rules — the scheduler layer above the processing engines.

title: Memory Management in DuckDB
author: Mark Raasveldt (DuckDB)
note: Shows how a single-node engine handles larger-than-memory batch jobs by streaming chunks and adaptively spilling joins, aggregations, and sorts to disk — a modern counterpoint to cluster-first thinking.
